prompts.label: What do I know about chair design?
prompts.capabilityDesc: 14-dimension classification across 3 platforms and 7 years of thinking
prompts.withoutVault: I don't have information about your chair design interests or background. Generally, chair design involves considerations of ergonomics, materials selection, structural engineering, and aesthetic form. Would you like me to explain any of these areas?
prompts.withVault:
You've been thinking about chair design since at least 2019 — early concept sketches linked to ideas like Encase, Plant, and Branch. By 2023, you had a concrete goal: design your own chair and enter the architects' chair competition. Your Apple Notes from that period show deep research into wood species (Japanese Zelkova, cypress, hickory), anticlastic curves, and makers like Thos. Moser and Bern Chandley.

Your design philosophy note frames the core tension you keep returning to: form follows function, or form embodies feeling? You haven't resolved that — it's a productive tension that shows up in the roundtable you ran with Thonet, Eames, Wegner, Prouve, and Rietveld.

By 2025 you were moving from thinking to making — tools list (spokeshave, travisher, scorp, band saw, lathe), 3D modeling in Fusion 360. The trajectory is clear: observer → student → builder. Seven years of notes, three platforms, one compounding interest.

results.title: Furniture
results.excerpt: Goal for this year — design my own chair. If it's anything close to what I imagine, consider registering to the architects chair competition by 11/1.

results.title: Design Philosophy
results.excerpt: Form follows? complements? Function. Form manifests? displays? embodies? Feelings/emotions. Function dictates? Form.

results.title: Chair
results.excerpt: Earliest concept — linked to Encase, Plant, Branch, Bag, Lace. The seed of the idea, years before the research began.

results.title: Build a chair
results.excerpt: Tools: moisture meter, drill/brace, spokeshave, travisher, scorp, band saw, lathe. Moving from thinking to making.

results.title: Modeling Bern Chandley chairs in Fusion 360
results.excerpt: Modeling the Harry, No. 14, and Lowbow chairs to practice 3D skills. Windsor construction: solid carved seat, round tenons, steam-bent components.

results.title: Learning chair design from historical masters
results.excerpt: A roundtable with Thonet, Eames, Wegner, Prouve, and Rietveld — first-principles chair design through the voices of the masters.

results.title: Chair Roundtable — Session 1
results.excerpt: Six legendary designers argue about ergonomics, materials, cost, and craft. The output of the roundtable conversation, structured as a learning artifact.

prompts.label: How did the agentic stack evolve?
prompts.capabilityDesc: Following the decision trail through connected notes
prompts.withoutVault: I'm not aware of the specifics of your agentic stack. In general, agentic architectures involve orchestrating AI models with tools, memory, and planning capabilities. Would you like me to explain common patterns?
prompts.withVault:
The agentic stack started as a concept in your March 23 session where you asked Claude to be 'chief architect' — that session produced the first four-layer model: shared state, data pipelines, surfaces, and session roles.

Four days later, the skill audit (March 27) consolidated 13 personas and mapped their interconnections. This surfaced a problem: skills were isolated, not composable. The interconnection map made dependencies explicit for the first time.

By April 1, the Five Flywheels document formalized what had been implicit: the system isn't a stack, it's five compounding loops. Session-to-state, learning-to-behavior, gap-to-ship, work-to-content, skill-to-coaching. Each one makes the next cycle smarter.

The key insight across this trail: the architecture wasn't designed once. It was discovered through iteration — each session revealed structure that was already there but unnamed.

nodes.title: Agentic Stack
nodes.excerpt: Daniel's mental model for building with AI. Infrastructure first, automation last. Four layers: shared state, data pipelines, surfaces, session roles.

nodes.title: Debrief — Agentic Stack Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Daniel asked Claude to be the 'chief architect' of his Claude Code setup. First formal architecture session — produced the four-layer model.

nodes.title: Skill Audit and Agentic Stack Visual Redesign
nodes.excerpt: Skills migrated and consolidated. 13 personas mapped. Surfaced the composability problem — skills were isolated, not interconnected.

nodes.title: Agentic Harness — Skill Interconnection Map
nodes.excerpt: Every skill's inputs, outputs, and dependencies mapped explicitly. Made the implicit structure visible.

nodes.title: Agentic Stack — Five Flywheels and I/O Map
nodes.excerpt: One flywheel is the pitch. This is the engineering diagram. Five compounding loops: session→state, learning→behavior, gap→ship, work→content, skill→coaching.

prompts.label: What's my composter business strategy?
prompts.capabilityDesc: Four different tools contributed different pieces of the answer
prompts.withoutVault: I don't have details about your composter business plans. If you'd like, I can help you think through a business strategy for a composting venture — what stage are you at?
prompts.withVault:
You researched the electric kitchen composter market in a deep Claude session — market sizing, regulatory tailwinds (NYC composting mandate, Mayor Mamdani resumed fines on day one), and competitive landscape of Asian brands with limited US presence.

You modeled three options across 24 months: Option A (pure distribution), Option B (distribution-to-white-label), Option C (B2B-led compliance packages targeting NYC property managers). Option C won on nearly every metric — highest revenue, highest net profit, lowest startup capex at roughly $19,600.

The PM decision from March 27 placed Composter as Tier 1 alongside Deadweight and the website. Your NorthStar frames it as a revenue venture. Notion tracks the current status: in-progress, with next actions on first outreach, OEM supplier research, pricing model, and legal entity formation.

The strategy is selected. The gap is execution — no outreach has happened yet.

sources.title: Electric kitchen composter US market opportunity
sources.excerpt: Market sizing, competitive landscape, three business options modeled with 24-month P&L. Option C (B2B-led compliance) selected — highest revenue, lowest capex at ~$19,600.

sources.title: Composter — General
sources.excerpt: Status: in-progress. Tier: 2. Next actions: first outreach, OEM supplier research, pricing model, legal entity formation.

sources.title: PM Decisions — 2026-03-27
sources.excerpt: Current Tier 1 is Deadweight, Composter, Website. All three need shipping actions, not infrastructure.

sources.title: NorthStar
sources.excerpt: B2B-led electric composter distribution targeting NYC composting mandate compliance. Revenue venture — bridge income for long-term furniture ambitions.
